items.md: I think that part of the cuts (probably one page) occur within or between published pages 2 and 3. It is also possible that the first cut is an unknown half page that follows published page 3. These cuts could involve consultation with the professors at the university or a gag about paying the tunnel workers or perhaps Scrooge's preparations for his own investigation after the workers flee, but this is strictly conjecture.
items.value: I think that part of the cuts (probably one page) occur within or between published pages 2 and 3. It is also possible that the first cut is an unknown half page that follows published page 3. These cuts could involve consultation with the professors at the university or a gag about paying the tunnel workers or perhaps Scrooge's preparations for his own investigation after the workers flee, but this is strictly conjecture.

items.md: There are two known unpublished half pages, numbered 5 (bottom) and 7 (top), and one would think that there should be a full page between them. Unfortunately, there are three published half pages that fit very neatly between them. This rules out a simple explanation of these cuts that would place the order as unpublished page 5 (bottom), then an unknown missing half page, published page 4 and the top of published page 5, the top of unpublished 7, and then the bottom of published page 5. It also necessitates a more complicated and less plausible explanation. The top half to published page 4 could originally have been the top half of page 5 and in cutting and revising, the panel 1 caption could have been expanded, and Donald added to the picture. The kids' dialogue in panel 1 and all of panel 2 could have been left unchanged. The dialogue in panels 3 and 4 and Donald's expression would have been changed. He would have been just helping
items.value: There are two known unpublished half pages, numbered 5 (bottom) and 7 (top), and one would think that there should be a full page between them. Unfortunately, there are three published half pages that fit very neatly between them. This rules out a simple explanation of these cuts that would place the order as unpublished page 5 (bottom), then an unknown missing half page, published page 4 and the top of published page 5, the top of unpublished 7, and then the bottom of published page 5. It also necessitates a more complicated and less plausible explanation. The top half to published page 4 could originally have been the top half of page 5 and in cutting and revising, the panel 1 caption could have been expanded, and Donald added to the picture. The kids' dialogue in panel 1 and all of panel 2 could have been left unchanged. The dialogue in panels 3 and 4 and Donald's expression would have been changed. He would have been just helping

items.md: Scrooge load the mine cart originally. In the half page numbered 5 (bottom), Scrooge has the supplies already loaded in the cart. Donald asks him who he plans to have accompany him down as an observer. Scrooge asks Donald if he has $500 and coerces Donald into accompanying him down, using a 1950 IOU for 50 cents that—at compound interest—now amounts to $500. This would explain the pained expression on Donald's face in panel 1 of page 4, a face that I think was clipped from elsewhere and added here. Next would come the bottom half of published page 4 but since it does not fit neatly in its present state, major revisions would have to have been made there. I can't think of a better explanation except that Barks numbered the 5 (bottom) half page wrong (perhaps it was 4), but I am inclined to rule out that possibility. I don't find this explanation of the origins of what is published as page 4 very satisfying.

I can't think of a better explanation except that Barks numbered the 5 (bottom) half page wrong (perhaps it was 4), but I am inclined to rule out that possibility. items.md: Published page 5 (top) fits perfectly after published page 4 (bottom) and before unpublished page 7 (top). In 7 (top) the kids ask rhetorically who could have pulled the cable pin since there was nobody and nothing there except those round rocks. They conjecture about invisible men — invisible jokers playing unfunny jokes. The bottom half of published page 5 (originally numbered as the bottom half of page 7) follows and the story is
